breakClass: DateTime arithmetic helper inside an unmaterialized query: DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays/AddMinutes (plain CLR helpers in Helpers/SendMailShim.cs, NOT registered EF functions) applied to a COLUMN expression inside a server-side LINQ-to-Entities projection or Where. EF6 used DbFunctions.AddDays (canonical -> SQL DATEADD); the port swapped in DbFunctionsCompat.* which EF Core 10 cannot translate, so the query throws 'could not be translated' -> HTTP 500. Fix = EF-native (col).Value.AddDays(n ?? 0) which DOES translate to DATEADD.
why: select new of unmaterialized db.servicereports query; .ToList() boundary at L7058 after projection -> server-side. CLR helper untranslatable -> 500. Used by sales-cost computation. Legacy (archive L8037) DbFunctions.AddDays. Port regression.
finalFix: In Controllers/SalesReportController.cs line 7056, replace the untranslatable CLR helper with the EF-native instance call so it translates to SQL DATEADD (mirrors legacy EF6 DbFunctions.AddDays and preserves the DateTime? result type): change `endtime = (a.endtime < a.starttime) ? DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays(a.endtime, 1) : a.endtime,` to `endtime = (a.endtime < a.starttime) ? a.endtime.Value.AddDays(1) : a.endtime,`. (`endtime` is DateTime? per Models/ProTask.cs:35; `.Value.AddDays(1)` translates to DATEADD(day,1,[endtime]) and the false branch keeps returning `a.endtime`, so output is identical.) Apply the same one-line fix to the twin at line 7087 in GetTotalEmpCost for completeness.
reasoning: I could not refute it; the flag is correct. (a) SERVER-SIDE confirmed: at Controllers/SalesReportController.cs:7056 the fragment `endtime = (a.endtime < a.starttime) ? DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays(a.endtime, 1) : a.endtime` lives inside `(from a in db.servicereports join b in db.servicereportmembers ... join c in db.Employees ... where addtas.Contains(a.protaskid) select new { a.starttime, endtime = ..., hourlyrate = ... }).ToList()` in method GetTotalEmpCostwithrate. All three sources are DbSets (ApplicationDbContext.cs:27/67/68 â€” Employees, servicereports, servicereportmembers), so the comprehension is a single composed IQueryable. The ONLY materialization boundary is the `.ToList()` at line 7058, which sits AFTER the `select new`; there is no `.ToList()`/`.AsEnumerable()`/`.AsAsyncEnumerable()` between the `from` and the `select`. So the projection (and the AddDays call in it) is part of the SQL-translated query, not client-side. It is not a standalone top-level local and not a group-aggregate over an IGrouping. (b) WOULD 500 confirmed: DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays (Helpers/SendMailShim.cs:70 `public static DateTime? AddDays(DateTime? date, int? days) => date?.AddDays(days ?? 0)`) is a plain CLR static method, NOT a registered EF function and NOT an EF.Functions method (EF Core has no EF.Functions.AddDays). EF Core 10 cannot translate an arbitrary static-method call embedded in a server-side projection's conditional â†’ InvalidOperationException 'could not be translated' â†’ HTTP 500. This fires on any matching row, i.e. realistic data, so HIGH severity (not edge-only). (c) PORT REGRESSION confirmed: legacy EF6 at _archive/legacy-mvc5/Controllers/SalesReportController.cs:8037 used `DbFunctions.AddDays(a.endtime, 1)`, the canonical EF6 function that maps to SQL DATEADD; the port swapped in the CLR shim. This is precisely the documented batch-26 'DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays/AddMinutes inside an unmaterialized query' class, whose established fix is the EF-native `(col).Value.AddDays(n)` form. (Note: the sibling GetTotalEmpCost at line 7087 has the identical defect and should be fixed the same way, though only line 7056 was the flagged target.)

breakClass: DateTime arithmetic helper inside an unmaterialized query: DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays/AddMinutes (plain CLR helpers in Helpers/SendMailShim.cs, NOT registered EF functions) applied to a COLUMN expression inside a server-side LINQ-to-Entities projection or Where. EF6 used DbFunctions.AddDays (canonical -> SQL DATEADD); the port swapped in DbFunctionsCompat.* which EF Core 10 cannot translate, so the query throws 'could not be translated' -> HTTP 500. Fix = EF-native (col).Value.AddDays(n ?? 0) which DOES translate to DATEADD.
why: select new of unmaterialized db.servicereports query; .ToList() boundary at L7088 after projection -> server-side. CLR helper untranslatable -> 500. Legacy (archive L8068) DbFunctions.AddDays. Port regression.
finalFix:
In Controllers/SalesReportController.cs, GetTotalEmpCost (L7070), replace L7087:

  endtime = (a.endtime < a.starttime) ? DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays(a.endtime, 1) : a.endtime

with the EF-native form that translates to SQL DATEADD:

  endtime = (a.endtime < a.starttime) ? a.endtime.Value.AddDays(1) : a.endtime

EF Core 10 translates DateTime.AddDays(...) to DATEADD, matching the legacy DbFunctions.AddDays behavior. `.Value` is safe because the ternary's true-branch is only taken when `a.endtime < a.starttime`, a comparison that is only true when endtime is non-null (in EF the SQL comparison excludes NULLs, and the in-memory consumer at L7092 already casts to (DateTime), assuming non-null). Output is preserved exactly (endtime shifted +1 day when it precedes starttime, i.e. an overnight job).

Apply the identical fix to the sibling GetTotalEmpCostwithrate at L7056 (same pattern, same server-side .ToList() projection, also reachable), changing `DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays(a.endtime, 1)` to `a.endtime.Value.AddDays(1)`.
reasoning:
I tried to refute this flag but every load-bearing claim holds up independently, so I confirm it.

LOCATION: Controllers/SalesReportController.cs line 7087, method `public double GetTotalEmpCost(long salesid, long? technician=0)` (method starts L7070). The flagged expression: `endtime = (a.endtime < a.starttime) ? DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays(a.endtime, 1) : a.endtime`.

(a) SERVER-SIDE â€” CONFIRMED. The projection is part of `var data = (from a in db.servicereports join b in db.servicereportmembers ... where addtas.Contains(a.protaskid) && (...) select new { a.starttime, endtime = ... }).ToList();`. The `.ToList()` sits at the END of the query chain (L7088); there is NO `.ToList()/.AsEnumerable()/.ToArray()` boundary inside the query before the `select new`. So the entire projection is composed into an IQueryable<servicereport>-derived expression and enumerated by `.ToList()` â€” it runs server-side. `db.servicereports` is a genuine mapped DbSet (ApplicationDbContext.cs L67: `public DbSet<servicereport> servicereports`). Entity `servicereport` (Models/ProTask.cs L28-44) has `DateTime? starttime` (L34) and `DateTime? endtime` (L35) as real mapped columns. This is NOT a standalone top-level local query that's exempt â€” exempt locals run client-side only when their terminal operator (FirstOrDefault etc.) is the WHOLE query; here the full Where+join+projection is sent to SQL. It is NOT an IGrouping aggregate.

(b) WOULD 500 â€” CONFIRMED (high). `DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays` is a plain CLR static helper, NOT a registered EF function (Helpers/SendMailShim.cs L67-71: `public static class DbFunctionsCompat { public static DateTime? AddDays(DateTime? date, int? days) => date?.AddDays(days ?? 0); ... }` with comment "EF Core has no EF.Functions.AddDays; client-evaluated"). EF Core 10 cannot translate an arbitrary CLR method call embedded in a server-side projection, so it throws InvalidOperationException "The LINQ expression ... could not be translated" â†’ HTTP 500. This fires whenever the method body reaches the query (task != 0) and the join yields rows â€” i.e., realistic data.

PORT REGRESSION â€” CONFIRMED. Legacy EF6 _archive/legacy-mvc5/Controllers/SalesReportController.cs L8051-8069 is structurally identical (same `.ToList()` at the end, same server-side projection) but uses `DbFunctions.AddDays(a.endtime, 1)` (L8068) â€” the EF6 canonical function that translates to SQL DATEADD. The port swapped the translatable canonical for the untranslatable CLR shim. Pure regression.

REACHABILITY â€” CONFIRMED. GetTotalEmpCost is called per-row from ~14 live report projections (e.g. L3413/3414, 4666/4667, 5181/5182, 6270/6271, 6785/6786). Those call sites sit AFTER `var vv = v.AsEnumerable().Select(o => new {...})` (e.g. L3385), so the calls execute client-side per row, and each call re-executes the inner db.servicereports query against SQL â€” exactly where the translation failure surfaces.

The sibling method GetTotalEmpCostwithrate (L7039) has the identical bug at L7056 (same DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays inside a server-side .ToList() projection) and is also reachable from the same report endpoints â€” worth fixing in the same pass, though the flag named only GetTotalEmpCost.

breakClass: left-join null member access / non-nullable value-column read on a possibly-null DefaultIfEmpty() entity in a server-side projection (EF Core 10 shaper reads SQL NULL into a non-nullable CLR value type â€” decimal/int/DateTime/enum â€” and throws; EF6 silently produced default(T))
why: Driving table is db.Items (ALL items); h=SEItems left join is NULL for any item never sold. SEItems.ItemUnitPrice/ItemQuantity are non-nullable; server-side (.Distinct()/.OrderBy/.Skip/.Take/.ToList later). Reading + multiplying NULL columns -> EF Core 10 500 whenever the catalog has an unsold item. where-clauses on k are all `==0||` gated so don't force h non-null. Legacy identical.
finalFix:
Output-preserving fix mirroring EF6 (NULL -> default(decimal) = 0), keeping unsold items in the result as legacy did. Guard `h` for the non-nullable members and recompute the derived figures from the guarded values. Replace the five flagged projection members (lines 10133-10135, 10142-10143):

    SellingPrice = h != null ? h.ItemUnitPrice : 0m,
    ItemQuantity = h != null ? h.ItemQuantity : 0m,
    sellingcost  = h != null ? h.ItemQuantity * h.ItemUnitPrice : 0m,
    ...
    purchasecost = h != null ? h.ItemQuantity * a.PurchasePrice : 0m,
    profit       = h != null ? (h.ItemQuantity * h.ItemUnitPrice) - (h.ItemQuantity * a.PurchasePrice) : 0m

Notes: (1) line 10134 currently uses the anonymous-member shorthand `h.ItemQuantity` (member name inferred as ItemQuantity) â€” it must be rewritten to the explicit `ItemQuantity = h != null ? h.ItemQuantity : 0m` so the property name is preserved for the JS/DataTables consumer. (2) The ternary `h != null ? ... : 0m` translates server-side in EF Core 10 to a CASE WHEN that yields 0 instead of reading NULL into the non-nullable decimal, reproducing EF6's default(decimal) exactly. (3) `e.ItemUnitName`/`f.ItemUnitName` (left joins on ItemUnits) are string (nullable-reference) and are safe â€” strings already shape NULL fine; no change needed there. (4) If product intent were to EXCLUDE unsold items, an alternative is adding `&& h != null` to the where-clause, but that changes the row set vs legacy; the guarded-projection above preserves the legacy output and is the minimal fix.
reasoning:
I attempted to refute and could not. The claim holds on every required point.

FILE/LOCATION: QuickNetCore/Controllers/SalesReportController.cs, method GetItemWisenew (signature at line 10065), projection lines 10133-10143.

(a) GENUINELY SERVER-SIDE â€” CONFIRMED. The query is built as an IQueryable: `var v = (from a in db.Items ...).Distinct();` then `v = v.OrderBy(b => b.SEDate);`. The first and only materialization boundary is `v.ToList()` at line 10152 (and `v.Count()` at 10153). The flagged select-new at 10122-10144 is composed straight into the SQL SELECT â€” no prior .ToList()/.ToArray()/.AsEnumerable() boundary, not a standalone top-level FirstOrDefault, and not a group-aggregate over an IGrouping. Server-side confirmed.

(b) WOULD 500 ON REALISTIC DATA â€” CONFIRMED. Driving table is db.Items (entire catalog). `h` is the SEItems side of `join h in db.SEItemss on a.ItemID equals h.Item into third / from h in third.DefaultIfEmpty()` â€” a left join, so `h` is NULL for any item that was never sold. SEItems.ItemUnitPrice and SEItems.ItemQuantity are non-nullable `decimal` (Models/SalesEntry.cs:350-351). The projection reads these non-nullable value members directly off the possibly-null `h`: `SellingPrice = h.ItemUnitPrice` (10133) and `h.ItemQuantity` (10134), plus the derived `sellingcost`/`purchasecost`/`profit`. In EF Core 10 the shaper reads SQL NULL into a non-nullable CLR decimal and throws InvalidOperationException ("The data is NULL... cannot be cast/read on NULL"), surfacing as a 500. The WHERE clause does NOT rescue it: every filter on k/l is `==0 ||` or `==\"\" ||` gated (SalesExecutive==0||..., Salesman==0||..., fromdate==\"\"||...), and the supplied filters are on k (SalesEntry), never on h â€” so unsold items with NULL h are NOT excluded and flow into the projection. With default report params (Brand=0, Category=0, SalesExecutive=0, Salesman=0, blank dates) the report selects ALL items; any catalog with even one never-sold item triggers the 500. That is realistic, not edge-only â†’ HIGH severity.

PORT REGRESSION â€” CONFIRMED. Legacy EF6 source at _archive/legacy-mvc5/Controllers/SalesReportController.cs lines 11483-11525 is identical in the projection (only difference is DbFunctions.DiffDays vs EF.Functions.DateDiffDay in the where-clause, irrelevant here). EF6's object materializer silently coerced the NULL non-nullable columns to default(decimal)=0; EF Core 10 throws. Worked in EF6, breaks in Core â€” genuine port regression.

I could not refute the hunter; verdict = confirmed.
